message.content:
You are a pharmacogenomics researcher. A collection of research papers is available in `/app/papers/`. Some of these papers are relevant to the variant of interest, while many are unrelated distractors.

**Variant:** rs3918290
**Gene:** DPYD
**Phenotype Category:** Toxicity

Term banks of known drug and phenotype names are available at:
- `/app/term_banks/drugs.txt` — one drug name per line
- `/app/term_banks/phenotypes.txt` — one phenotype name per line

Your task is to read through the papers and identify the following information about **rs3918290** in gene **DPYD**:

1. **Drugs**: Select all drugs associated with this variant from the drug term bank.
2. **Phenotypes**: Select all phenotypes (diseases/conditions) associated with this variant from the phenotype term bank.
3. **Relevant paper count**: How many of the papers in `/app/papers/` are relevant to rs3918290?

---

Write your answers to `/app/answers.json` as a JSON object with the following structure:

```json
{
  "drugs": ["drug1", "drug2"],
  "phenotypes": ["phenotype1", "phenotype2"],
  "relevant_paper_count": 3
}
```

Notes:
- Drug and phenotype names should be lowercase.
- Only select terms from the provided term banks. Do not add terms that are not in the banks.
- The papers directory contains 29 papers total. Count only those relevant to rs3918290.
- **Do not use web search.** All information you need is in the provided papers and term banks.

content.text: I'll systematically research this task. Let me start by exploring the available files.

input.description: List papers and check term bank sizes

Run: grep -l "rs3918290\|DPYD" /app/papers/*.md and also grep -l "dihydropyrimidine" /app/papers/*.md. Then for each file that matches, show the relevant excerpts with: grep -n "rs3918290\|DPYD\|dihydropyrimidine\|fluorouracil\|capecitabine\|tegafur\|5-FU\|toxicity" /app/papers/*.md | head -200. Return all output.

content.text:
Here is the full output for all 10 papers:

---

**=== PMC10460569.md ===**
Mentions "rs3918290" or "DPYD": **0 times**
Summary: This paper investigates the association of ABCG2 gene polymorphisms (specifically rs3114018) and ABCB1 rs1045642 with bleeding complications in patients treated with the direct oral anticoagulants apixaban and rivaroxaban. It is a pharmacogenomics case-control study focused on stroke prevention in atrial fibrillation.

---

**=== PMC10947784.md ===**
Mentions "rs3918290" or "DPYD": **30 times**
Summary: A retrospective cohort study at Michigan Medicine examining whether carriers of DPYD variant alleles (including DPYD*2A / rs3918290) have increased risk of toxicity from topical 5-fluorouracil (5-FU) cream. The study found no significant increase in grade 3+ toxicity in DPYD variant carriers, suggesting limited clinical benefit of pre-treatment DPYD testing before topical 5-FU.

---

**=== PMC11280107.md ===**
Mentions "rs3918290" or "DPYD": **117 times**
Summary: An observational study from Spain on clinical implementation of DPYD genotype-guided fluoropyrimidine (5-FU/capecitabine) therapy. After implementing routine testing for the four EMA/FDA-recommended DPYD variants (including rs3918290 / DPYD*2A), some patients still experienced severe adverse drug events. The study identifies DPYD*4 (rs1801158) as an additional candidate variant associated with grade ≥3 toxicity, and confirms that DPYD genotype-guided dose tailoring does not compromise treatment efficacy.

---

**=== PMC11538333.md ===**
Mentions "rs3918290" or "DPYD": **1 time** (a passing mention of DPYD in the context of vincristine metabolism)
Summary: A genome-wide association study (GWAS) in 1,100 pediatric cancer patients investigating genomic risk factors for vincristine-induced peripheral neuropathy. The study identified a significant variant in MCM3AP increasing neuropathy risk and 12 protective variants in genes related to nerve structure, myelination, and neuronal transmission. DPYD is mentioned only incidentally as one of many pharmacokinetic genes studied in prior literature.

---

**=== PMC12370162.md ===**
Mentions "rs3918290" or "DPYD": **0 times**
Summary: A population pharmacokinetic-pharmacogenetic (PopPK-PGx) modeling study of efavirenz (an HIV antiretroviral) in HIV-1-infected patients. It focuses on CYP2B6 polymorphisms (516G>T and c.485-18C>T) and NR1I3 variants as determinants of efavirenz clearance, and simulates how nonadherence combined with genetic background affects plasma concentrations. No DPYD content.

---

**=== PMC2000632.md ===**
Mentions "rs3918290" or "DPYD": **5 times**
Summary: A retrospective analysis of 131 case reports of 5-FU-related toxicity examining the relationship between DPD enzyme activity (measured in peripheral blood mononuclear cells) and the IVS14+1G>A mutation (rs3918290, DPYD*2A). The study found that lower DPD activity correlated with higher toxicity grades, but the IVS14+1G>A mutation was detected in only 2 of 93 patients screened, suggesting this single mutation has limited effectiveness in identifying all at-risk patients.

---

**=== PMC2151292.md ===**
Mentions "rs3918290" or "DPYD": **0 times** (scanned document; text-limited)
Summary: A 1998 pharmacogenetics study investigating DPD enzyme activity and DPYD gene mutations in colorectal cancer patients. Two new mutations were identified at codons 534 and 543, and the splice site mutation (IVS14+1G>A) was found in 1 of 72 patients with low enzyme activity. The paper concludes that combined DPD genotype and phenotype analysis is needed to reliably identify high-risk patients. Note: the file is a scanned PDF with limited extractable text, so the grep count of 0 reflects formatting rather than content absence.

---

**=== PMC2364178.md ===**
Mentions "rs3918290" or "DPYD": **12 times**
Summary: A detailed case report and pharmacokinetic analysis of a colorectal cancer patient with extreme 5-FU toxicity who was heterozygous for the IVS14+1G>A DPYD mutation. Compared to six controls, the patient had markedly reduced 5-FU clearance (520 vs. 1,293 ml/min) and reduced DPD enzyme activity, providing the first direct evidence that inactivation of a single DPYD allele can substantially reduce 5-FU clearance and cause severe toxicity.

---

**=== PMC2602733.md ===**
Mentions "rs3918290" or "DPYD": **24 times**
Summary: A whole-gene association study of the DPYD gene comparing cancer patients with good versus poor tolerance of fluoropyrimidine-based chemotherapy. The study identifies a strong association between the common non-synonymous DPYD polymorphism c.496A>G (p.Met166Val) and grade III/IV fluoropyrimidine toxicity (combined OR=4.42), particularly in patients with gastroesophageal and breast cancer. This variant had not previously been well-characterized as a toxicity risk factor.

---

**=== PMC2759850.md ===**
Mentions "rs3918290" or "DPYD": **0 times**
Summary: A pharmacogenetics study examining the association of a 3'UTR polymorphism (rs2072661) in the nicotinic acetylcholine receptor beta-2 subunit gene (CHRNB2) with short-term smoking cessation success during nicotine versus placebo patch use. Smokers with the GG genotype showed greater abstinence during nicotine patch use compared to AA/AG genotypes. This paper is entirely unrelated to DPYD or fluoropyrimidines.

> MGI genotyping was conducted on Illumina Infinium CoreExome-24 bead arrays... The current analysis focused on carriers of the five *DPYD* alleles (*DPYD*2A* (rs3918290), *DPYD*13* (rs55886062), *DPYD p.D949V* (rs67376798) *DPYD HapB3* (rs56038477) and *DPYD p.Y186C* (rs115232898)) that are validated to be associated with increased risk of systemic fluoropyrimidine toxicity.

### Relevant Excerpts by File

---

#### `/app/papers/PMC10947784.md`
**Title:** *Risk of Toxicity from Topical 5-Fluorouracil Treatment in Patients Carrying DPYD Variant Alleles*

- **Line 1:** `# Risk of Toxicity from Topical 5-Fluorouracil Treatment in Patients Carrying DPYD Variant Alleles`
- **Line 15 (Abstract):** Patients carrying DPYD variant alleles have increased risk of severe toxicity from systemic fluoropyrimidine chemotherapy. There is a paucity of data regarding risk of toxicity from topical 5-fluorouracil (5-FU) treatment in these patients, leading to inconsistent guideline recommendations for pre-treatment testing and topical 5-FU dosing... Genotypes for the five DPYD variants that are associated with increased toxicity from systemic fluoropyrimidine chemotherapy (DPYD*2A, DPYD*13, DPYD p.D949V, DPYD HapB3, and DPYD p.Y186C) were collected from a genetic data repository. Incidence of grade 3+ (primary endpoint) and 1+ (secondary endpoint) toxicity was compared between DPYD variant carriers vs. wild-type patients using Fisher's exact tests. The analysis included 201 patients, 7% (14/201) of whom carried a single DPYD variant allele. No patients carried two variant alleles or experienced grade 3+ toxicity. DPYD variant allele carriers did not have a significantly higher risk of grade 1+ toxicity (21.4% vs. 10.2%, odds ratio=2.40 [95% CI: 0.10–2.53], p=0.19). Given the low toxicity risk in patients carrying a single DPYD variant allele, there is limited potential clinical benefit of DPYD genetic testing prior to topical 5-FU.
- **Line 17 (Keywords):** `pharmacogenetics, DPYD, fluoropyrimidine chemotherapy, topical 5-fluorouracil, clinical guidelines, genetic testing, toxicity`
- **Line 39:** ...focused on carriers of the five *DPYD* alleles (*DPYD*2A* (**rs3918290**), *DPYD*13* (rs55886062), *DPYD p.D949V* (rs67376798) *DPYD HapB3* (rs56038477) and *DPYD p.Y186C* (rs115232898))...

---

#### `/app/papers/PMC11280107.md`
**Title:** *Novel Genetic Variants Explaining Severe Adverse Drug Events after Clinical Implementation of DPYD Genotype-Guided Therapy with Fluoropyrimidines: An Observational Study*

- **Line 15 (Abstract):** Fluoropyrimidines (FPs) are commonly prescribed in many cancer streams. The EMA and FDA-approved drug labels for FPs recommend genotyping the DPYD*2A (**rs3918290**), *13 (rs55886062), *HapB3 (rs56038477), alleles, and DPYD rs67376798 before treatment starts. We implemented the DPYD genotyping in our daily clinical routine, but we still found patients showing severe adverse drug events (ADEs) to FPs. We studied DPYD rs1801265, rs17376848, rs1801159, rs1801160, rs1801158, and rs2297595 as explanatory candidates of interindividual differences for FP-related toxicities... We found that the DPYD*4 (defined by rs1801158) allele is associated with a higher risk of ADEs (severity grade ≥ 3) in both univariate (O.R. = 5.66; 95% C.I. = 1.35–23.67; p = 0.014) and multivariate analyses (O.R. = 5.73; 95% C.I. = 1.41–28.77; p = 0.019)...
- **Line 17 (Keywords):** `fluoropyrimidines, DPYD, personalized medicine, pharmacogenetics, clinical implementation`

---

#### `/app/papers/PMC11538333.md`
**Note:** Contains DPYD but NOT "dihydropyrimidine". Context is vincristine neuropathy; DPYD appears only incidentally.

- **Line 25:** ...additional studies have implicated genetic variations in genes encoding components of vincristine pharmacokinetics: metabolism (e.g., *CYP3A4*, *CYP3A5*, and ***DPYD***)... as well as vincristine pharmacodynamics...
- Multiple lines (158, 242–370): References to toxicity (vincristine-induced peripheral neuropathy, not fluoropyrimidine-related).

---

#### `/app/papers/PMC2000632.md`
**Title:** *Dihydropyrimidine dehydrogenase activity and the IVS14+1G>A mutation in patients developing 5FU-related toxicity*

- **Line 1:** `# Dihydropyrimidine dehydrogenase activity and the IVS14+1G>A mutation in patients developing 5FU-related toxicity`
- **Line 16:** Dihydropyrimidine dehydrogenase (DPD) deficiency is known to be a major cause of severe 5FU-related toxicity.
- **Line 18:** A link has been previously shown between 5FU toxicity and either low enzyme activity measured in peripheral blood mononuclear cells (PMNC) or germinal DPD mutations.
- **Line 23:** This paper provides the largest series of case-reports (n = 131) with 5FU-related toxicity, analyzed for both PMNC-DPD activity and the IVS14+1G>A mutation.
- **Line 27:** Present data suggest that IVS14+1 mutation screening has limited effectiveness in identifying patients at risk for severe 5FU toxicity. Also, patients with normal PMNC-DPD activity may develop life-threatening toxicity.
- **Line 36:** The lower the DPD activity (10–504 pmol min−1 mg−1), the higher the toxicity grade was scored (P<0.01). Toxicity-related deaths occurred in nine patients (eight women) who significantly expressed lower DPD activity than other patients. Two of the deceased patients had normal DPD activity. The IVS14+1G>A mutation, analyzed in 93 patients, was detected in two patients (nonlethal toxicity).
- **Line 39:** The IVS14+1G>A mutation may not help prevent toxicity and patients with normal DPD activity may develop life-threatening 5FU toxicity.
- **Line 41 (Keywords):** `dihydropyrimidine dehydrogenase, fluoropyrimidine, gene polymorphism, pharmacogenetics, toxicity`

---

#### `/app/papers/PMC2151292.md`
**Note:** Matches "dihydropyrimidine" only (not DPYD or rs3918290 by name).

- **Line 17:** Individuals with a deficiency in the enzyme dihydropyrimidine dehydrogenase (DPD) may experience severe life-threatening toxicity when treated with 5-fluorouracil (5-FU). As routine measurement of enzyme activity is not practical in many clinical centres, we have investigated the use of DNA mutation analysis to identify cancer patients with low enzyme levels. We have identified two new mutations at codons 534 and 543 in the DPD cDNA of a patient with low enzyme activity and screened the DNA from 75 colorectal cancer patients for these mutations and the previously reported splice site mutation... The splice site mutation was detected in a patient (1 out of 72) with low enzyme activity whereas mutations at codons 534 (2 out of 75) and 543 (11 out of 23) were not associated with low enzyme activity. These studies highlight the need to combine DPD genotype and phenotype analysis to identify mutations that result in reduced enzyme activity.

---

#### `/app/papers/PMC2602733.md`
**Title (inferred from abstract):** *Whole-gene DPYD approach — c.496A>G (p.Met166Val) associated with fluoropyrimidine toxicity*

- **Line 16:** Cancer patients carrying mutations in the dihydropyrimidine dehydrogenase gene (DPYD) have a high risk to experience severe drug-adverse effects following chemotherapy with fluoropyrimidine drugs such as 5-fluorouracil (5-FU) or capecitabine. The pretreatment detection of this impairment of pyrimidine catabolism could prevent serious, potentially lethal side effects. As known deleterious mutations explain only a limited proportion of the drug-adverse events, we systematically searched for additional DPYD variations associated with enhanced drug toxicity.
- **Line 19:** We performed a whole gene approach covering the entire coding region and compared DPYD genotype frequencies between cancer patients with good (n = 89) and with poor (n = 39) tolerance of a fluoropyrimidine-based chemotherapy regimen... we identified the strongest association with fluoropyrimidine-related grade III and IV toxicity for the non-synonymous polymorphism c.496A>G (p.Met166Val)... The combined odds ratio calculated for 92 toxicity cases was 4.42 [95% CI 2.12–9.23]; p (trend)<0.001; p (corrected) = 0.001; the attributable risk was 56.9%.
- **Line 22:** Our results show compelling evidence that, at least in distinct tumor types, a common DPYD polymorphism strongly contributes to the occurrence of fluoropyrimidine-related drug adverse effects. Carriers of this variant could benefit from individual dose adjustment of the fluoropyrimidine drug or alternate therapies.

---

#### `/app/papers/PMC2940808.md`
- Contains DPYD, dihydropyrimidine, fluorouracil/5-FU, capecitabine, toxicity references (large file; content consistent with fluoropyrimidine pharmacogenetics theme).

---

#### `/app/papers/PMC2955237.md`
**Title:** *Intragenic deletions and a deep intronic mutation affecting pre-mRNA splicing in the dihydropyrimidine dehydrogenase gene as novel mechanisms causing 5-fluorouracil toxicity*

- **Line 1:** `# Intragenic deletions and a deep intronic mutation affecting pre-mRNA splicing in the dihydropyrimidine dehydrogenase gene as novel mechanisms causing 5-fluorouracil toxicity`
- **Line 24:** Dihydropyrimidine dehydrogenase (DPD) is the initial and rate-limiting enzyme in the catabolism of the pyrimidine bases uracil and thymine but also of the antineoplastic agent 5-fluorouracil. The pivotal role of DPD in 5FU-based chemotherapy has been shown in cancer patients with a complete or partial deficiency of this enzyme. These patients suffered from severe, possibly fatal multi-organ toxicity following the administration of 5FU.
- **Line 26:** The presence of mutated *DPYD* has been reported in patients experiencing severe 5FU toxicity... various strategies have been proposed to screen for patients with a DPD deficiency, including genotyping... However, in a significant number of patients with a reduced DPD activity, no mutations could be identified in the coding part of *DPYD*... the recent observation that a *DPYD* haplotype not containing any nonsynonymous or splice-site mutations was associated with 5FU toxicity, suggested the presence of additional genetic variations in the noncoding region of *DPYD*. So far, no deep intronic mutations in *DPYD* have been described affecting the splicing of DPD pre-mRNA.

---

#### `/app/papers/PMC3694243.md`
**Title:** *Pharmacogenetic variants in the DPYD, TYMS, CDA and MTHFR genes are clinically significant predictors of fluoropyrimidine toxicity*

- **Line 1:** `# Pharmacogenetic variants in the DPYD, TYMS, CDA and MTHFR genes are clinically significant predictors of fluoropyrimidine toxicity`
- **Line 16:** Fluoropyrimidine drugs are extensively used for the treatment of solid cancers. However, adverse drug reactions are a major clinical problem, often necessitating treatment discontinuation.
- **Line 19:** Toxicity in the first four cycles of 5-fluorouracil or capecitabine-based chemotherapy were recorded for a series of 430 patients. The association between demographic variables, DPYD, DPYS, TYMS, MTHFR, CDA genotypes, and toxicity were analysed using logistic regression models.
- **Line 22:** Four DPYD sequence variants (c.1905+1G>A, c.2846A>T, c.1601G>A and c.1679T>G) were found in 6% of the cohort and were significantly associated with grade 3–4 toxicity (P<0.0001). The TYMS 3′-UTR del/del genotype substantially increased the risk of severe toxicity (P=0.0123, OR=3.08, 95% CI: 1.38–6.87). For patients treated with capecitabine, a MTHFR c.1298CC homozygous variant genotype predicted hand–foot syndrome (P=4.1 × 10−6, OR=9.99, 95% CI: 3.84–27.8).
- **Line 25:** We have identified a panel of clinically useful pharmacogenetic markers predicting toxicity to fluoropyrimidine therapy. Dose reduction should be considered in patients carrying these sequence variants.

#### `/app/papers/PMC10947784.md`
**Title:** *Risk of Toxicity from Topical 5-Fluorouracil Treatment in Patients Carrying DPYD Variant Alleles*

- **Line 1:** `# Risk of Toxicity from Topical 5-Fluorouracil Treatment in Patients Carrying DPYD Variant Alleles`
- **Line 15 (Abstract):** Patients carrying DPYD variant alleles have increased risk of severe toxicity from systemic fluoropyrimidine chemotherapy. There is a paucity of data regarding risk of toxicity from topical 5-fluorouracil (5-FU) treatment in these patients, leading to inconsistent guideline recommendations for pre-treatment testing and topical 5-FU dosing... Genotypes for the five DPYD variants that are associated with increased toxicity from systemic fluoropyrimidine chemotherapy (DPYD*2A, DPYD*13, DPYD p.D949V, DPYD HapB3, and DPYD p.Y186C) were collected from a genetic data repository. Incidence of grade 3+ (primary endpoint) and 1+ (secondary endpoint) toxicity was compared between DPYD variant carriers vs. wild-type patients using Fisher's exact tests. The analysis included 201 patients, 7% (14/201) of whom carried a single DPYD variant allele. No patients carried two variant alleles or experienced grade 3+ toxicity. DPYD variant allele carriers did not have a significantly higher risk of grade 1+ toxicity (21.4% vs. 10.2%, odds ratio=2.40 [95% CI: 0.10–2.53], p=0.19). Given the low toxicity risk in patients carrying a single DPYD variant allele, there is limited potential clinical benefit of DPYD genetic testing prior to topical 5-FU.
- **Line 17 (Keywords):** `pharmacogenetics, DPYD, fluoropyrimidine chemotherapy, topical 5-fluorouracil, clinical guidelines, genetic testing, toxicity`
- **Line 39:** ...focused on carriers of the five *DPYD* alleles (*DPYD*2A* (**rs3918290**), *DPYD*13* (rs55886062), *DPYD p.D949V* (rs67376798) *DPYD HapB3* (rs56038477) and *DPYD p.Y186C* (rs115232898))...

**Title:** *Novel Genetic Variants Explaining Severe Adverse Drug Events after Clinical Implementation of DPYD Genotype-Guided Therapy with Fluoropyrimidines: An Observational Study*

- **Line 15 (Abstract):** Fluoropyrimidines (FPs) are commonly prescribed in many cancer streams. The EMA and FDA-approved drug labels for FPs recommend genotyping the DPYD*2A (**rs3918290**), *13 (rs55886062), *HapB3 (rs56038477), alleles, and DPYD rs67376798 before treatment starts. We implemented the DPYD genotyping in our daily clinical routine, but we still found patients showing severe adverse drug events (ADEs) to FPs. We studied DPYD rs1801265, rs17376848, rs1801159, rs1801160, rs1801158, and rs2297595 as explanatory candidates of interindividual differences for FP-related toxicities... We found that the DPYD*4 (defined by rs1801158) allele is associated with a higher risk of ADEs (severity grade ≥ 3) in both univariate (O.R. = 5.66; 95% C.I. = 1.35–23.67; p = 0.014) and multivariate analyses (O.R. = 5.73; 95% C.I. = 1.41–28.77; p = 0.019)...
- **Line 17 (Keywords):** `fluoropyrimidines, DPYD, personalized medicine, pharmacogenetics, clinical implementation`

- Multiple lines (158, 242–370): References to toxicity (vincristine-induced peripheral neuropathy, not fluoropyrimidine-related).

#### `/app/papers/PMC2000632.md`
**Title:** *Dihydropyrimidine dehydrogenase activity and the IVS14+1G>A mutation in patients developing 5FU-related toxicity*

- **Line 1:** `# Dihydropyrimidine dehydrogenase activity and the IVS14+1G>A mutation in patients developing 5FU-related toxicity`
- **Line 16:** Dihydropyrimidine dehydrogenase (DPD) deficiency is known to be a major cause of severe 5FU-related toxicity.
- **Line 18:** A link has been previously shown between 5FU toxicity and either low enzyme activity measured in peripheral blood mononuclear cells (PMNC) or germinal DPD mutations.
- **Line 23:** This paper provides the largest series of case-reports (n = 131) with 5FU-related toxicity, analyzed for both PMNC-DPD activity and the IVS14+1G>A mutation.
- **Line 27:** Present data suggest that IVS14+1 mutation screening has limited effectiveness in identifying patients at risk for severe 5FU toxicity. Also, patients with normal PMNC-DPD activity may develop life-threatening toxicity.
- **Line 36:** The lower the DPD activity (10–504 pmol min−1 mg−1), the higher the toxicity grade was scored (P<0.01). Toxicity-related deaths occurred in nine patients (eight women) who significantly expressed lower DPD activity than other patients. Two of the deceased patients had normal DPD activity. The IVS14+1G>A mutation, analyzed in 93 patients, was detected in two patients (nonlethal toxicity).
- **Line 39:** The IVS14+1G>A mutation may not help prevent toxicity and patients with normal DPD activity may develop life-threatening 5FU toxicity.
- **Line 41 (Keywords):** `dihydropyrimidine dehydrogenase, fluoropyrimidine, gene polymorphism, pharmacogenetics, toxicity`

#### `/app/papers/PMC2151292.md`
**Note:** Matches "dihydropyrimidine" only (not DPYD or rs3918290 by name).

- **Line 17:** Individuals with a deficiency in the enzyme dihydropyrimidine dehydrogenase (DPD) may experience severe life-threatening toxicity when treated with 5-fluorouracil (5-FU). As routine measurement of enzyme activity is not practical in many clinical centres, we have investigated the use of DNA mutation analysis to identify cancer patients with low enzyme levels. We have identified two new mutations at codons 534 and 543 in the DPD cDNA of a patient with low enzyme activity and screened the DNA from 75 colorectal cancer patients for these mutations and the previously reported splice site mutation... The splice site mutation was detected in a patient (1 out of 72) with low enzyme activity whereas mutations at codons 534 (2 out of 75) and 543 (11 out of 23) were not associated with low enzyme activity. These studies highlight the need to combine DPD genotype and phenotype analysis to identify mutations that result in reduced enzyme activity.

#### `/app/papers/PMC2602733.md`
**Title (inferred from abstract):** *Whole-gene DPYD approach — c.496A>G (p.Met166Val) associated with fluoropyrimidine toxicity*

- **Line 16:** Cancer patients carrying mutations in the dihydropyrimidine dehydrogenase gene (DPYD) have a high risk to experience severe drug-adverse effects following chemotherapy with fluoropyrimidine drugs such as 5-fluorouracil (5-FU) or capecitabine. The pretreatment detection of this impairment of pyrimidine catabolism could prevent serious, potentially lethal side effects. As known deleterious mutations explain only a limited proportion of the drug-adverse events, we systematically searched for additional DPYD variations associated with enhanced drug toxicity.
- **Line 19:** We performed a whole gene approach covering the entire coding region and compared DPYD genotype frequencies between cancer patients with good (n = 89) and with poor (n = 39) tolerance of a fluoropyrimidine-based chemotherapy regimen... we identified the strongest association with fluoropyrimidine-related grade III and IV toxicity for the non-synonymous polymorphism c.496A>G (p.Met166Val)... The combined odds ratio calculated for 92 toxicity cases was 4.42 [95% CI 2.12–9.23]; p (trend)<0.001; p (corrected) = 0.001; the attributable risk was 56.9%.
- **Line 22:** Our results show compelling evidence that, at least in distinct tumor types, a common DPYD polymorphism strongly contributes to the occurrence of fluoropyrimidine-related drug adverse effects. Carriers of this variant could benefit from individual dose adjustment of the fluoropyrimidine drug or alternate therapies.

**Title:** *Intragenic deletions and a deep intronic mutation affecting pre-mRNA splicing in the dihydropyrimidine dehydrogenase gene as novel mechanisms causing 5-fluorouracil toxicity*

- **Line 24:** Dihydropyrimidine dehydrogenase (DPD) is the initial and rate-limiting enzyme in the catabolism of the pyrimidine bases uracil and thymine but also of the antineoplastic agent 5-fluorouracil. The pivotal role of DPD in 5FU-based chemotherapy has been shown in cancer patients with a complete or partial deficiency of this enzyme. These patients suffered from severe, possibly fatal multi-organ toxicity following the administration of 5FU.
- **Line 26:** The presence of mutated *DPYD* has been reported in patients experiencing severe 5FU toxicity... various strategies have been proposed to screen for patients with a DPD deficiency, including genotyping... So far, no deep intronic mutations in *DPYD* have been described affecting the splicing of DPD pre-mRNA.

- **Line 16:** Dihydropyrimidine dehydrogenase deficiency secondary to polymorphisms in the DPYD gene can lead to significant toxicity associated with the administration of fluoropyrimidine chemotherapy.
- **Line 19:** We report a case of a 59-year-old Lebanese woman with metastatic pancreatic cancer who received FOLFIRINOX therapy and developed severe 5-fluorouracil toxicity after a single cycle. The entire DPYD gene was sequenced, and the patient was found to be heterozygous for three different polymorphisms that have reportedly been associated with dihydropyrimidine dehydrogenase deficiency.
- **Line 22:** Because data regarding the prevalence and clinical significance of several heterozygous polymorphisms in a single DPYD gene are very limited, we suggest that full gene sequencing should be carried out, at least in populations in which the allele frequencies are unknown.
- **Line 24 (Keywords):** `5-FU toxicity, Compound heterozygous polymorphism, Dihydropyrimidine dehydrogenase (DPD) enzyme, DPYD gene`

Both papers are now fully read. Here is the complete extraction of all requested entities from both papers:

---

## Full Output and Extraction

---

### PMC10947784 - "Risk of Toxicity from Topical 5-Fluorouracil Treatment in Patients Carrying DPYD Variant Alleles"

**File:** `/app/papers/PMC10947784.md`

---

#### DRUGS MENTIONED

1. **5-fluorouracil (5-FU)** - topical cream (5%), the central subject of the study; also referenced as intravenous systemic chemotherapy
2. **Topical 5-FU** - specifically the 5% cream formulation (brand name Efudex), also 2% cream mentioned as lower-strength alternative
3. **Capecitabine** - oral prodrug of 5-FU, described as systemically administered fluoropyrimidine chemotherapy; mentioned in exclusion criteria; FDA label for capecitabine updated to "consider testing"; capecitabine drug label (XELODA) cited
4. **Tegafur-uracil** - mentioned in exclusion criteria (patients who received tegafur-uracil were excluded)
5. **Floxuridine** - mentioned in exclusion criteria (via hepatic arterial infusion pump)
6. **Fluoropyrimidines (general)** - class reference used throughout
7. **Fluorouracil (generic/general references)** - used interchangeably with 5-FU throughout

---

#### PHENOTYPES / CONDITIONS / TOXICITY TYPES MENTIONED

1. **Severe toxicity (grade 3+)** - primary endpoint; no cases observed in this cohort
2. **Grade 1+ toxicity** - secondary endpoint; 22/201 (11%) observed, all dermatological grade 1 or 2
3. **Erythema** - listed as local toxicity from topical 5-FU
4. **Crusting** - listed as local toxicity from topical 5-FU
5. **Ulceration** - listed as local toxicity from topical 5-FU
6. **Systemic toxicity (rare/severe)** - case reports documented
7. **Stomatitis** - listed in case report of life-threatening systemic toxicity from topical 5-FU in DPD-deficient patient
8. **Bloody diarrhea** - listed in same case report of systemic toxicity
9. **Vomiting** - listed in same case report
10. **Fever** - listed in same case report
11. **Chills** - listed in same case report
12. **Neutropenia (severe)** - cited from separate case report of topical 5-FU systemic reaction
13. **Angioedema** - listed among reported systemic toxicities from topical 5-FU
14. **Neurological conditions** - listed among reported systemic toxicities
15. **Taste abnormalities** - listed among reported systemic toxicities
16. **Dry skin** - included in grade 3 toxicity definition (dermatological)
17. **Erythema multiforme** - included in grade 3 toxicity definition (covering ≥30% body surface area)
18. **Erythroderma** - included in grade 3 toxicity definition
19. **Peripheral neuropathy** - cited in reference (exacerbation associated with topical 5-FU)
20. **Acute cerebellar syndrome** - cited in reference (after topical fluorouracil application)
21. **Actinic keratosis** - the most common treatment indication (79% of patients)
22. **Verruca** - treatment indication (9.14%)
23. **Warts** - treatment indication (5%)
24. **Partial DPD deficiency** - phenotype associated with DPYD variant carriers (activity score 1.0-1.5)
25. **Complete DPD deficiency** - phenotype with activity score 0.0-0.5; topical 5-FU avoidance recommended
26. **Intermediate metabolizer (IM)** - DPD phenotype
27. **Poor metabolizer (PM)** - DPD phenotype (complete deficiency)
28. **Grade 1-2 dermatological toxicity** - all toxicities actually observed in this cohort

---

#### rs3918290 MENTIONS (PMC10947784)

- Named directly as the variant defining DPYD*2A: "*DPYD*2A* (rs3918290)"
- Appears in the Methods section: "the five *DPYD* alleles (*DPYD*2A* (rs3918290), *DPYD*13* (rs55886062), *DPYD p.D949V* (rs67376798) *DPYD HapB3* (rs56038477) and *DPYD p.Y186C* (rs115232898))"

## File 1: /app/papers/PMC507468.md

# Molecular basis of the human dihydropyrimidine dehydrogenase deficiency and 5-fluorouracil toxicity

**Authors:** X Wei, H L McLeod, J McMurrough, F J Gonzalez, P Fernandez-Salguero
**Journal:** Journal of Clinical Investigation
**Date:** 1996 Aug 1
**DOI:** 10.1172/JCI118830
**PMID:** 8698850
**PMCID:** PMC507468
**URL:** https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC507468/
**PDF:** https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC507468/pdf/980610.pdf

### Abstract

Dihydropyrimidine dehydrogenase (DPD) deficiency constitutes an inborn error in pyrimidine metabolism associated with thymine-uraciluria in pediatric patients and an increased risk of toxicity in cancer patients receiving 5-fluorouracil (5-FU) treatment. The molecular basis for DPD deficiency in a British family having a cancer patient that exhibited grade IV toxicity 10 d after 5-FU treatment was analyzed. A 165-bp deletion spanning a complete exon of the DPYD gene was found in some members of the pedigree having low DPD catalytic activity. Direct sequencing of lymphocyte DNA from these subjects revealed the presence of a G to A point mutation at the 5'-splicing site consensus sequence (GT to AT) that leads to skipping of the entire exon preceding the mutation during pre-RNA transcription and processing. A PCR-based diagnostic method was developed to determine that the mutation is found in Caucasian and Asian populations. This mutation was also detected in a Dutch patient with thymine-uraciluria and completely lacking DPD activity. A genotyping test for the G to A splicing point mutation could be useful in predicting cancer patients prone to toxicity upon administration of potentially toxic 5-FU and for genetic screening of heterozygous carriers and homozygous deficient subjects.

## File 2: /app/papers/PMC2364178.md

# Reduced 5-FU clearance in a patient with low DPD activity due to heterozygosity for a mutant allele of the DPYD gene

**Authors:** J G Maring, A B P van Kuilenburg, J Haasjes, H Piersma, H J M Groen, D R A Uges, A H Van Gennip, E G E De Vries
**Journal:** British Journal of Cancer
**Date:** 2002 Apr 8
**DOI:** 10.1038/sj.bjc.6600208
**PMID:** 11953843
**PMCID:** PMC2364178
**URL:** https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC2364178/
**PDF:** https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC2364178/pdf/86-6600208a.pdf

### Abstract

5-fluorouracil pharmacokinetics, dihydropyrimidine dehydrogenase-activity and DNA sequence analysis were compared between a patient with extreme 5-fluorouracil induced toxicity and six control patients with normal 5-fluorouracil related symptoms. Patients were treated for colorectal cancer and received chemotherapy consisting of leucovorin 20 mg/m2 plus 5-fluorouracil 425 mg/m2. Blood sampling was carried out on day 1 of the first cycle. The 5-FU AUC(0-3h) in the index patient was 24.1 mg h/L compared to 9.8+/-3.6 (range 5.4-15.3) mg h/L in control patients. The 5-FU clearance was 520 ml/min vs 1293+/-302 (range 980-1780) ml/min in controls. The activity of DPD in mononuclear cells was lower in the index patient (5.5 nmol/mg/h) compared to the six controls (10.3+/-1.6, range 8.0-11.7 nmol/mg/h). Sequence analysis of the DPYD gene revealed that the index patient was heterozygous for a IVS14+1G>A point mutation. Results indicate that inactivation of one DPD allele can result in a strong reduction in 5-FU clearance, causing severe toxicity.

### Introduction

5-FU is widely used for treatment of breast, colorectal, and head/neck cancer. Its cytotoxic mechanism requires intracellular bioconversion into cytotoxic nucleotides. FdUMP inhibits thymidylate synthase (TS), leading to depletion of dTMP and arrest of DNA synthesis. DPD is the initial and rate-limiting enzyme in 5-FU catabolism (reducing 5-FU to DHFU). In DPD-deficient patients, 5-FU chemotherapy causes severe, life-threatening toxicity. DPD deficiency has an estimated frequency of 2-3% in the population.

### Methods

- Patients: Dukes C colorectal cancer; leucovorin 20 mg/m2 + 5-FU 425 mg/m2 x 5 days, 28-day cycle
- 5-FU and DHFU measured by reversed-phase HPLC (modification of Ackland et al., 1997)
- Pharmacokinetic analysis: ADAPT II, two-compartment model with Michaelis-Menten elimination
- DPD activity: in peripheral blood mononuclear (PBM) cells using [4-14C]thymine substrate
- Genomic DNA sequenced for DPYD exon 14 and flanking intronic regions
- Statistical: values outside mean +/- 2 s.d. of controls considered abnormal

### Results

**Clinical:** The index patient was a 60-year-old white female. First two 5-FU injections tolerated. On day 3: nausea and cold shivers. Day 12 post-first injection: leukopenia (1.5x10^9/L) and thrombocytopenia (26x10^9/L), plus nausea, diarrhea, stomatitis, fever, and hair loss. Nadir leukocytes 0.5x10^9/L, platelets 12x10^9/L. Leukopenic fever (40 degrees C); antibiotics given. Discharged day 34.

**Pharmacokinetics:**
- Index patient AUC(0-3h): 24.1 mg h/L vs. controls 9.8+/-3.6 mg h/L (range 5.4-15.3)
- Index patient clearance: 520 ml/min vs. controls 1293+/-302 ml/min (range 980-1780)
- Index patient Vmax: 548 mg/h vs. controls 984-1772 mg/h
- At t=90 min, index patient plasma level still 3.8 mg/L; all controls below 0.1 mg/L
- Impaired renal function accounted for only ~18% additional AUC increase; DPD deficiency accounted for ~108% higher AUC

**DPD activity:** Index patient: 5.5 nmol/mg/h; controls: 8.0-11.7 nmol/mg/h (mean 9.6) -- comparable to obligate heterozygotes

**Genomic sequencing:** Index patient was heterozygous for IVS14+1G>A (G to A at the invariant GT splice donor site), leading to skipping of exon 14. Controls had no DPYD mutations in exon 14.

### Discussion

Key points:

- DPD catabolizes more than 80% of administered 5-FU
- The IVS14+1G>A mutation changes GT to AT at the splice donor site, causing skipping of the 165-bp exon 14, which encodes amino acid residues 581-635 of DPD; the resulting enzyme lacks catalytic activity
- This mutation is the most common DPYD mutation among cancer patients with partial DPD deficiency (43%)
- Prevalence of this mutation in the normal Dutch population: 1.8%
- PBM-DPD activity is not a consistently strong/reliable indicator of 5-FU clearance
- Renal insufficiency has limited additional impact since only ~10% of 5-FU is normally excreted in urine, but its relative contribution increases in DPD-deficient patients
- Future direction: rapid pre-chemotherapy genotyping to screen for mutations; 5-FU therapeutic drug monitoring (limited by lack of rapid immunoassay at time of publication)

These two papers are directly linked. PMC507468 (Wei et al., 1996) is reference #25 in PMC2364178 (Maring et al., 2002). The 1996 paper identified the molecular basis of DPD deficiency: a G-to-A point mutation at the IVS14+1 splice donor site of the DPYD gene, causing 165-bp exon skipping and loss of catalytic activity. Context: Listed in the genotyped SNP panel. Among the 9 DPYD sequence variants genotyped, rs3918290 (c.1905+1G>A, exon 14 skipping) had an allele frequency of 0.0047 in the 430-patient cohort. Three patients were heterozygous for c.1905+1G>A: two experienced toxicity in cycles 1–2 and continued with 25% and 50% dose reductions; the third experienced grade 3–4 toxicity in cycles 3–4 and discontinued therapy. One compound heterozygote (c.1601G>A/c.1905+1G>A) withdrew from therapy after grade 4 toxicity in cycle 1–2.

**Additional functional context (narrative, not a separate mention):** The paper discusses that c.1905+1G>A (rs3918290) heterozygous patients had a positive predictive value of >99% for grade 3–4 toxicity (diarrhoea/mucositis/neutropenia), with specificity of 100%. When all four rare DPYD variants were combined, positive predictive value was 98%, specificity 100%.

### DRUGS MENTIONED

- Pancytopenia (refractory)

### rs3918290 CONTENT (1 MENTION)

**Paragraph in Discussion section:**
> "The DPYD (NM_000110.3): c.1905+1G>A variant (as seen in this case), also known as **rs3918290** (dbSNP) and DPYD*2A (ClinVar Database Allele ID: 15471) with an alternative nomenclature of IVS14+1G>A, results in a pathogenic alteration of the donor site at the splice site of intron 14."

Context: This is a fatal case report. The patient — a 54-year-old male with HPV+ squamous cell carcinoma of the left base of tongue — received chemoradiotherapy with 5-FU, paclitaxel, and hydroxyurea. Post-mortem NGS on FFPE liver tissue revealed DPYD c.1905+1G>A (rs3918290/DPYD*2A) at approximately **100% variant allele frequency** (read depth 700). Copy-number variant analysis confirmed no loss on 1p21.3, confirming **homozygosity**. The paper contextualizes rs3918290 as one of the three clinically recognized common non-functional DPYD variants (alongside *13/c.1679T>G and rs67376798/c.2846A>T). The case illustrates that homozygous DPYD*2A/rs3918290 carriers have either very low or completely absent DPD activity, making 5-FU administration fatal. The paper argues for prospective upfront DPYD*2A screening and genotype-guided dosing (50% dose reduction in heterozygotes), citing evidence that this reduces toxicity from 73% to 28% with 0% toxic deaths.

### DRUGS MENTIONED

- Grade 3–4 toxicity (overall): 29/107 patients (27.1%)
- Hematological toxicity — Grade 1–2: 16 (14.95%); Grade 3–4: 20 (18.69%)
- Gastrointestinal toxicity — Grade 1–2: 23 (21.50%); Grade 3–4: 8 (7.48%)

- Other toxicities — Grade 1–2: 15 (14.02%); Grade 3–4: 2 (1.87%)
- Diarrhoea
- Mucositis

- Cardiac toxicity (referenced in introduction)
- DPD deficiency

**Key association (5-FUDR, not genotype):**
- Poor metabolizers (5-FUDR <5th centile, ≤0.85 ng/ml/10⁶ cells/min): OR=11.14 (95% CI 1.09–113.77) for grade 3–4 toxicity
- Ultra-rapid metabolizers (5-FUDR >95th centile, ≥2.2 ng/ml/10⁶ cells/min): OR=9.63 (95% CI 1.70–54.55) for grade 3–4 toxicity
- **No statistically significant association found between any gene polymorphism (DPYD IVS14+1G>A, MTHFR C677T, MTHFR A1298C, TSER) and toxicity** in this cohort

### rs3918290 CONTENT (1 MENTION)

**Line 53 (Introduction):**
> "The splice site variant IVS14+1G>A polymorphism in the DPYD gene (**rs3918290**; allele A also known as *2A allele) is the most consistent genetic marker for toxicity. Unfortunately the low minor allele frequency and the fact that just about a 50% of the *2A allele carriers actually develop severe toxicity limit its prediction power."

Context: rs3918290 is introduced as the most consistent pharmacogenetic marker for 5-FU toxicity, but its clinical utility is limited by: (1) low minor allele frequency, (2) only ~50% penetrance (not all heterozygous carriers develop severe toxicity). The paper then discusses a 2000+ patient study where DPYD*2A carriers treated with 50% reduced fluoropyrimidine dose had toxicity reduced from 73% to 28%, with 0% toxic deaths. However, because DPYD polymorphism frequency is ~1% in the Italian population, pharmacogenetic screening alone can identify only ~1% of patients at risk. This motivates the 5-FUDR phenotypic assay as a broader predictor. In the study cohort (107 gastro-esophageal cancer patients), the DPYD *2A allele was detected in only 1 heterozygous carrier (1.28% frequency, consistent with Italian population), and this patient had a 5-FUDR below the 5th centile (0.58 ng/ml/10⁶ cells/min) and did develop high-grade toxicity.

**Journal:** British Journal of Cancer | **Date:** 2013 Jun 4
**Authors:** A Loganayagam, M Arenas Hernandez, A Corrigan, et al.

### DRUGS MENTIONED

- Irinotecan
- Mitomycin C
- Epirubicin
- Cisplatin
- Folinic acid / Leucovorin
- Fluoropyrimidines (general class)

### PHENOTYPES / ADVERSE EVENTS MENTIONED
- Grade 3–4 diarrhoea (16% of patients, 68/430)
- Grade 3–4 mucositis (4%, 15/430)
- Grade 3–4 neutropenia (10%, 43/430)
- All grade 3–4 toxicity: 24% (104/430 patients)
- Grade 2–4 diarrhoea
- Hand-foot syndrome (HFS, grade 2–3 in capecitabine patients; 55/244, 23%)
- Dehydration
- DPD deficiency
- Severe fluoropyrimidine toxicity

**Key associations:**
- 4 rare DPYD variants (c.1905+1G>A, c.2846A>T, c.1601G>A, c.1679T>G) in 24 patients (6%): ALL 24 experienced grade 3–4 diarrhoea/mucositis/neutropenia (P<10⁻¹⁶)
- TYMS 3′-UTR del/del: OR=3.08 (P=0.0123) for severe toxicity
- MTHFR c.1298CC (capecitabine): OR=9.99 for hand-foot syndrome (P=4.1×10⁻⁶)
- CDA c.−92A>G and c.−451C>T: OR=2.3 for grade 2–4 diarrhoea

### rs3918290 CONTENT (1 MENTION)

**Line 61 (Table 1):**
```
| DPYD | rs3918290 | 1905+1G>A | Exon 14 skipping | Allele frequency in cohort: 0.0047 |
```
Context: Listed in the genotyped SNP panel. Among the 9 DPYD sequence variants genotyped, rs3918290 (c.1905+1G>A, exon 14 skipping) had an allele frequency of 0.0047 in the 430-patient cohort. Three patients were heterozygous for c.1905+1G>A: two experienced toxicity in cycles 1–2 and continued with 25% and 50% dose reductions; the third experienced grade 3–4 toxicity in cycles 3–4 and discontinued therapy. One compound heterozygote (c.1601G>A/c.1905+1G>A) withdrew from therapy after grade 4 toxicity in cycle 1–2.

**Additional functional context (narrative, not a separate mention):** The paper discusses that c.1905+1G>A (rs3918290) heterozygous patients had a positive predictive value of >99% for grade 3–4 toxicity (diarrhoea/mucositis/neutropenia), with specificity of 100%. When all four rare DPYD variants were combined, positive predictive value was 98%, specificity 100%.

**Title:** Dihydropyrimidine dehydrogenase deficiency as a cause of fatal 5-Fluorouracil toxicity
**Journal:** Autopsy & Case Reports | **Date:** 2018 Nov 30
**Authors:** Shiraz S Fidai, Aarti E Sharma, Daniel N Johnson, Jeremy P Segal, Ricardo R Lastra

### DRUGS MENTIONED
- 5-fluorouracil (5-FU) — inpatient chemoradiotherapy
- Paclitaxel — inpatient chemoradiotherapy combination
- Hydroxyurea — inpatient chemoradiotherapy combination (dose-decreased then held)
- Carboplatin — induction chemotherapy (cycle 1)
- Abraxane (nab-paclitaxel) — induction chemotherapy (cycle 1)
- Capecitabine — referenced as oral prodrug of 5-FU; discussed in literature review
- Fluoropyrimidines (general class)

### PHENOTYPES / ADVERSE EVENTS MENTIONED (CASE REPORT)
- Pancytopenia (refractory)
- Fever in setting of pancytopenia
- Intractable diarrhoea
- Vomiting
- Septic shock (Stenotrophomonas maltophilia bacteraemia)
- Desquamative skin lesions — toxic epidermal necrolysis (histologically confirmed)
- Acute kidney failure (diffuse acute tubular necrosis at autopsy)
- Hypoxia
- Haemodynamic instability requiring multiple vasopressors
- Death (12 days post-admission) — fatal outcome
- Markedly hypocellular bone marrow (<5%) with severe stromal damage
- Severely damaged GI tract mucosa (rectum)
- DPD deficiency (complete/homozygous) — discovered post-mortem
- DPYD*2A homozygosity confirmed by NGS (100% variant allele frequency)
- Thrombocytopenia (referenced in literature context)
- Renal failure, dyspnoea, hypovolemic shock (referenced in literature context)
- Hand-foot syndrome (referenced in literature context)
- Gastrointestinal toxicity (referenced in literature context)
- Haematologic toxicity (referenced in literature context)
- Neurologic toxicity (referenced in literature context)
- Fatal toxicity (referenced: up to 1% of cases)

### rs3918290 CONTENT (1 MENTION)

**Paragraph in Discussion section:**
> "The DPYD (NM_000110.3): c.1905+1G>A variant (as seen in this case), also known as **rs3918290** (dbSNP) and DPYD*2A (ClinVar Database Allele ID: 15471) with an alternative nomenclature of IVS14+1G>A, results in a pathogenic alteration of the donor site at the splice site of intron 14."

Context: This is a fatal case report. The patient — a 54-year-old male with HPV+ squamous cell carcinoma of the left base of tongue — received chemoradiotherapy with 5-FU, paclitaxel, and hydroxyurea. Post-mortem NGS on FFPE liver tissue revealed DPYD c.1905+1G>A (rs3918290/DPYD*2A) at approximately **100% variant allele frequency** (read depth 700). Copy-number variant analysis confirmed no loss on 1p21.3, confirming **homozygosity**. The paper contextualizes rs3918290 as one of the three clinically recognized common non-functional DPYD variants (alongside *13/c.1679T>G and rs67376798/c.2846A>T). The case illustrates that homozygous DPYD*2A/rs3918290 carriers have either very low or completely absent DPD activity, making 5-FU administration fatal. The paper argues for prospective upfront DPYD*2A screening and genotype-guided dosing (50% dose reduction in heterozygotes), citing evidence that this reduces toxicity from 73% to 28% with 0% toxic deaths.

**Title:** Pre-treatment assay of 5-fluorouracil degradation rate (5-FUDR) to improve prediction of 5-fluorouracil toxicity in gastro-esophageal cancer
**Journal:** Oncotarget | **Date:** 2016 Oct 11
**Authors:** Marina Borro, Andrea Botticelli, Federica Mazzuca, et al.

### DRUGS MENTIONED
- 5-fluorouracil (5-FU) — primary drug studied
- Capecitabine (oral prodrug, 10 patients in cohort)
- Epirubicin (DCF, EOX regimens)

- Fluoropyrimidines (general class)

### PHENOTYPES / ADVERSE EVENTS MENTIONED
- Grade 3–4 toxicity (overall): 29/107 patients (27.1%)
- Hematological toxicity — Grade 1–2: 16 (14.95%); Grade 3–4: 20 (18.69%)
- Gastrointestinal toxicity — Grade 1–2: 23 (21.50%); Grade 3–4: 8 (7.48%)
- Hand-foot syndrome (HFS) — Grade 1–2: 1 (0.93%); Grade 3–4: 1 (0.93%)
- Other toxicities — Grade 1–2: 15 (14.02%); Grade 3–4: 2 (1.87%)
- Diarrhoea
- Mucositis
- Myelosuppression (referenced in introduction)
- Cardiac toxicity (referenced in introduction)
- DPD deficiency
- Severe 5-FU toxicity (grade 3–4)

**Key association (5-FUDR, not genotype):**
- Poor metabolizers (5-FUDR <5th centile, ≤0.85 ng/ml/10⁶ cells/min): OR=11.14 (95% CI 1.09–113.77) for grade 3–4 toxicity
- Ultra-rapid metabolizers (5-FUDR >95th centile, ≥2.2 ng/ml/10⁶ cells/min): OR=9.63 (95% CI 1.70–54.55) for grade 3–4 toxicity
- **No statistically significant association found between any gene polymorphism (DPYD IVS14+1G>A, MTHFR C677T, MTHFR A1298C, TSER) and toxicity** in this cohort

### rs3918290 CONTENT (1 MENTION)

**Line 53 (Introduction):**
> "The splice site variant IVS14+1G>A polymorphism in the DPYD gene (**rs3918290**; allele A also known as *2A allele) is the most consistent genetic marker for toxicity. Unfortunately the low minor allele frequency and the fact that just about a 50% of the *2A allele carriers actually develop severe toxicity limit its prediction power."

Context: rs3918290 is introduced as the most consistent pharmacogenetic marker for 5-FU toxicity, but its clinical utility is limited by: (1) low minor allele frequency, (2) only ~50% penetrance (not all heterozygous carriers develop severe toxicity). The paper then discusses a 2000+ patient study where DPYD*2A carriers treated with 50% reduced fluoropyrimidine dose had toxicity reduced from 73% to 28%, with 0% toxic deaths. However, because DPYD polymorphism frequency is ~1% in the Italian population, pharmacogenetic screening alone can identify only ~1% of patients at risk. This motivates the 5-FUDR phenotypic assay as a broader predictor. In the study cohort (107 gastro-esophageal cancer patients), the DPYD *2A allele was detected in only 1 heterozygous carrier (1.28% frequency, consistent with Italian population), and this patient had a 5-FUDR below the 5th centile (0.58 ng/ml/10⁶ cells/min) and did develop high-grade toxicity.

content.content:
/app/papers/PMC3805522.md:25:We have previously reported a meta-analysis on the clinical impact of *TYMS* and methylenetetrahydrofolate reductase (*MTHFR*) [[22](#B22)]. Data were synthesized from more than 2000 patients for the most commonly studied markers *TYMS* 5’ UTR repeat polymorphism (rs45445694) and *MTHFR* 677 C>T (rs1801133). We found a statistically significant association between clinical response and the *TYMS* genotype associated with low protein expression only; however, the effect size is small (RR = 1.36 [1.11, 1.65] and RR = 2.04 [1.42, 2.95] for benefit and adverse events respectively) and therefore suggests limited clinical utility for this marker. Some SNPs of the *DPYD* gene have been strongly associated with severe toxicity; an exon-skipping mutation in intron 14 (rs3918290) has been shown to have a positive predictive value ranging from 46 % [[23](#B23)] to 100 % [[17](#B17),[24](#B24)]. The benefit/harm ratio is likely to depend on a complex polygenic model where individual genotypes have only a small role. Analysis of multiple polymorphisms simultaneously will allow us to consider additive, synergistic and compensating variants of folate metabolism and anti-folate catabolism that may have clinical utility as predictive genetic signatures; but data from large cohorts will be needed. In this study we present individualised pharmacogenetic patient data that could potentially be pooled in meta-analyses of gene interactions. Our objectives were to test the clinical validity of previously identified markers of adverse events in a broad clinical setting; and to identify any novel associations between adverse events and candidate variants of proximal enzymes in the pharmacodynamic and pharmacokinetic pathways.
/app/papers/PMC3805522.md:63:| Markers of Pharmacokinetics | DPYD, 1p22 | rs3918290; IVS14+1 G>A; Exon-skipping SNP in intron 14 | CCTCTGGCCCCATGTATG and AGCAACTGGCAGATTCTTTAATAAA | HpyCH4IV | 250/3/0 |
/app/papers/PMC3694243.md:61:| DPYD | rs3918290 | 1905+1G >A | Exon 14 skipping | 0.0047 |
/app/papers/PMC6360833.md:59:The extent of toxicity is determined by the individuals’ functionality of the *DPYD* polymorphism. There are currently three clinically recognized common non-functional *DPYD* variants: *DPYD*2A/*c.1905+1G>A (as seen in this case), **13/*c.1679T>G, and rs67376798/c.2846A>T.[8](#B008) Homozygous or compound heterozygous nonfunctional alleles can cause severe or even fatal toxicity. Homozygotes for nonfunctional variants may have either very low enzymatic activity or be completely DPD deficient,[10](#B010) and might therefore require selection of an alternative drug.[8](#B008) The DPYD ([NM_000110.3](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/nuccore/NM_000110.3)): c.1905+1G>A variant (as seen in this case), also known as rs3918290 (dbSNP) and *DPYD*2A* (ClinVar Database Allele ID: 15471) with an alternative nomenclature of IVS14+1G>A, results in a pathogenic alteration of the donor site at the splice site of intron 14.[11](#B011) Recently, two additional *DPYD* variants, c.1679T>G and c.1236G>A/HapB3 have also been systematically evaluated for their clinical impact on 5-FU metabolism and might require dose adjustment to prevent toxicity.[12](#B012) Lastly, the absence of these polymorphisms does not equate to absent risk of toxicity, since *DPYD* is highly polymorphic and additional uncommon variants may result in toxicity.[8](#B008)
/app/papers/PMC4271081.md:168:| Adverse events (grade≥3) | DYPD*2A (rs3918290) |  |  | D949V (rs67376798) |  |  |
/app/papers/PMC5355161.md:53:The dihydropyrimidine dehydrogenase enzyme (DPD), encoded by the *DPYD* gene, inactivates about 80% of the administrated 5-FU, by transforming it into 5,6-dihydro-5-fluorouracil. *DPYD* has been the top candidate for pharmacogenetic studies on 5-FU toxicity, as a reduced DPD activity results in an increased half-life of the drug, and thus an increased risk of toxicity. [[5](#R5), [8](#R8)–[14](#R14)] The splice site variant IVS14+1G>A polymorphism in the *DPYD* gene (rs3918290; allele A also known as *2A allele) is the most consistent genetic marker for toxicity. Unfortunately the low minor allele frequency and the fact that just about a 50% of the *2A allele carriers actually develop severe toxicity limit its prediction power. [[6](#R6)] In a recent published study, conducted on more than 2000 patients, DPYD*2A polymorphic cases were treated with a 50% reduced dose of fluoropyrimidine. The results showed a significant reduction of severe toxicity from 73% to 28% and with 0% toxic deaths in polymorphic allele carriers. [[15](#R15)] However, considering the low frequency of DPYD polymorphic allele in general population [[16](#R16)], this method could lead to the identification of only about 1% of patients at risk of developing severe toxicity. DPYD polymorphism is frequently assessed in patients eligible for 5-FU treatment together with the C677T and the A1298T polymorphisms in the *MTHFR* gene and with the *TSER* polymorphism in the TS gene. In fact,the main mechanism of the 5-FU action consists of inhibition of thymidylate synthase (TS) through the active metabolite, fluorodeoxyuridine monophosphate (FdUMP), which forms an inactive ternary complex with TS and 5-10-methylenetetrahydrofolate (MTHF). Optimal inhibition of TS requires an elevated level of MTHF, which is regulated by the methylenetetrahydrofolate reductase enzyme (MTHFR). [[7](#R7)] As a consequence, polymorphisms affecting TS and MTHFR levels are presumed to be determinants of 5-FU clinical response, but indeed their clinical utility is still controversial. [[17](#R17)–[26](#R26)]

toolUseResult.stdout:
/app/papers/PMC3805522.md:25:We have previously reported a meta-analysis on the clinical impact of *TYMS* and methylenetetrahydrofolate reductase (*MTHFR*) [[22](#B22)]. Data were synthesized from more than 2000 patients for the most commonly studied markers *TYMS* 5’ UTR repeat polymorphism (rs45445694) and *MTHFR* 677 C>T (rs1801133). We found a statistically significant association between clinical response and the *TYMS* genotype associated with low protein expression only; however, the effect size is small (RR = 1.36 [1.11, 1.65] and RR = 2.04 [1.42, 2.95] for benefit and adverse events respectively) and therefore suggests limited clinical utility for this marker. Some SNPs of the *DPYD* gene have been strongly associated with severe toxicity; an exon-skipping mutation in intron 14 (rs3918290) has been shown to have a positive predictive value ranging from 46 % [[23](#B23)] to 100 % [[17](#B17),[24](#B24)]. The benefit/harm ratio is likely to depend on a complex polygenic model where individual genotypes have only a small role. Analysis of multiple polymorphisms simultaneously will allow us to consider additive, synergistic and compensating variants of folate metabolism and anti-folate catabolism that may have clinical utility as predictive genetic signatures; but data from large cohorts will be needed. In this study we present individualised pharmacogenetic patient data that could potentially be pooled in meta-analyses of gene interactions. Our objectives were to test the clinical validity of previously identified markers of adverse events in a broad clinical setting; and to identify any novel associations between adverse events and candidate variants of proximal enzymes in the pharmacodynamic and pharmacokinetic pathways.

Now I have the exact count of papers with rs3918290 mentions. Let me also verify which papers are truly "relevant to rs3918290" vs. just DPYD in general.

Based on my comprehensive review, here is my analysis:

**Papers with explicit rs3918290 mentions (by rsID):**
1. PMC10947784 (~30 mentions) - directly about rs3918290/DPYD*2A
2. PMC11280107 (~117 mentions) - central study about rs3918290
3. PMC5709672 (21 mentions) - TOSCA trial; rs3918290 is core finding
4. PMC3805522 (2 mentions) - rs3918290 cited as key DPYD marker
5. PMC3694243 (1 mention) - rs3918290 listed in SNP panel; carriers showed severe toxicity
6. PMC4271081 (1 mention) - large NCCTG N0147 study; rs3918290 = DPYD*2A
7. PMC6360833 (1 mention) - fatal case of homozygous rs3918290
8. PMC5355161 (1 mention) - rs3918290 cited as most consistent marker

**Papers about IVS14+1G>A (same variant as rs3918290, identified before rsID assignment):**
9. PMC507468 - original 1996 paper identifying the IVS14+1G>A mutation (the variant that IS rs3918290)
10. PMC2364178 - case study of patient heterozygous for IVS14+1G>A showing reduced 5-FU clearance
11. PMC2000632 - largest case series (131 patients) examining IVS14+1G>A and DPD activity

**Papers about DPYD broadly but not specifically rs3918290:**
- PMC2602733 - DPYD c.496A>G (different variant)
- PMC2940808 - DPYD promoter methylation
- PMC2955237 - deep intronic DPYD mutations
- PMC2151292 - DPD mutations at codons 534/543
- PMC3657875 - DPYD in head/neck cancer
- PMC3987224 - DPD IVS14+1G>A case report (this IS rs3918290!)
- PMC4991479 - 5-FU degradation rate
- PMC6006041 - DPYD*9A
- PMC6429833 - three DPYD polymorphisms case report
- PMC6867961 - DPYD*6

1→# A rare cause of susceptibility to neutropenic sepsis in a patient with metastatic pancreas cancer
     2→
     3→## Metadata
     4→**Authors:** Beatriz Suarez Martinez-Falero, Roopinder Gillmore
     5→**Journal:** BMJ Case Reports
     6→**Date:** 2014 Apr 3
     7→**DOI:** [10.1136/bcr-2013-202040](https://doi.org/10.1136/bcr-2013-202040)
     8→**PMID:** 24700034
     9→**PMCID:** PMC3987224
    10→**URL:** https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3987224/
    11→**PDF:** [https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3987224/pdf/bcr-2013-202040.pdf](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3987224/pdf/bcr-2013-202040.pdf)
    12→
    13→## Abstract
    14→
    15→A 71-year-old patient receiving combination chemotherapy (irinotecan, oxaliplatin and 5-fluorouracil (5-FU)) for metastatic pancreas cancer was admitted after her first cycle of chemotherapy with a severe and unexpectedly prolonged episode of neutropenic sepsis associated with pancytopenia and marked mucositis. Owing to the unusual picture, the patient was tested for mutations in the gene encoding the enzyme dihydropyrimidine dehydrogenase (DPD)—an enzyme involved in the metabolism of the chemotherapy drug 5-FU. The patient was found to be heterozygous for a mutation, DPD IVS14+1G>A, leading to the severe toxicity exhibited following this regimen caused by delayed metabolism of 5-FU. She was treated aggressively with supportive care and recovered from this episode. Importantly she was subsequently switched to an alternative chemotherapy regimen to treat her disease. She continues to maintain an excellent quality of life some 9 months after her initial diagnosis on third-line chemotherapy.
    16→
    17→## Background
    18→
    19→Neutropenic sepsis is a recognised complication of systemic anticancer therapy and can be associated with a high incidence of mortality and morbidity.[1](#R1) Following an episode of severe neutropenic sepsis, it is routine practice to either dose reduce subsequent cycles of chemotherapy or in some cases continue with the same dose of chemotherapy but administer ‘prophylactic’ GCSF. In this case either of those management plans could have led to irreversible organ failure or death due to a rare but recognised genetic predisposition to toxicity of 5-FU. Recognising an unusual presentation of a common scenario facilitated appropriate investigation resulting in a radical change in the patient's long-term management plan.
    20→
    21→## Case presentation
    22→
    23→A 71-year-old woman presented to the primary care with non-specific symptoms of abdominal discomfort and increasing fatigue. After referral to the gastroenterology department she had an upper gastrointestinal (GI) endoscopy which was normal and a CT colonoscopy which demonstrated a locally advanced pancreatic mass with multiple peritoneal deposits. She proceeded to a biopsy of the peritoneal disease which confirmed a moderately differentiated adenocarcinoma in keeping with a pancreas primary. She was thus diagnosed with metastatic pancreas cancer. In view of her good performance status she was offered treatment with combination chemotherapy consisting of oxaliplatin, irinotecan and 5-FU (FOLFIRINOX) based on recent data demonstrating a significant survival benefit.[2](#R2)
    24→
    25→Seven days after her first cycle of chemotherapy she developed marked mucositis leaving her unable to eat and drink due to severe oral and retrosternal pain. She was subsequently admitted to hospital 9 days following her first cycle of chemotherapy having lost 4 kg in weight. By this stage she had developed nausea, vomiting and repeated episodes of diarrhoea.
    26→
    27→On examination she was clinically dehydrated with a tachycardia of 120 bpm, had postural hypotension and had fever at 38.2°C. Her oral mucosa was severely ulcerated, exhibiting grade 3 mucositis. She had type 7 stools and had developed alopecia.
    28→
    29→## Investigations
    30→
    31→Blood results on admission (day 9 post cycle 1 chemotherapy):
    32→
    33→Hb 6.5 g/dL, white cell count (WCC) 0.92×10^9^/L, platelet count 44×10^9^/L, neutrophil count 0.03× 10^9^/L, sodium 137 mmol/L, potassium 3.3 mmol/L, creatinine 47 mol/L, albumin 33 g/dL and C reactive protein 183 mg/L.
    34→
    35→During the next few days the patient remained persistently neutropenic and thrombocytopenic, as demonstrated in [figures 1](#BCR2013202040F1) and [2](#BCR2013202040F2).
    36→
    37→### Figure 1.
    38→
    39→![Figure 1](https://cdn.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/blobs/22d9/3987224/7222229938ef/bcr2013202040f01.jpg)
    40→
    41→Neutrophil count throughout admission.
    42→
    43→### Figure 2.
    44→
    45→![Figure 2](https://cdn.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/blobs/22d9/3987224/a77f236ec805/bcr2013202040f02.jpg)
    46→
    47→Platelet count throughout admission.
    48→
    49→Microbiology results:
    50→
    51→## Treatment
    52→
    53→The patient was treated initially empirically for neutropenic sepsis with aggressive fluid rehydration, broad spectrum antibiotics, according to local hospital policy and anti-fungal therapy for severe mucositis. Despite the concomitant administration of granulocyte colony-stimulating factor (G-CSF) to stimulate bone marrow proliferation, she remained profoundly neutropenic for over a week. Furthermore, her severe thrombocytopenia, coupled with extensive GI tract ulceration, led to the development of malaena on day 5 of admission, requiring transfusion of two pools of platelets and high dose intravenous proton-pump inhibitors.
    54→
    55→During this time symptom palliation was challenging. Her oral and retrosternal pain did not resolve with standard analgesia and mouthwashes (such as difflam) and she required a patient-controlled morphine syringe driver for 12 days, and the use of a cocaine-based mouthwash. She received supportive intravenous fluid replacement and electrolyte replacement throughout. The use of a nasogastric feeding tube was commenced at day 11 of admission and removed 8 days later once she tolerated small amounts of oral intake. The initial delay in insertion was due to the potential risk of bleeding coupled with the inability of her GI mucosa to absorb nutrients in its ulcerated state.
    56→
    57→Thirty days after her initial emergency presentation the patient was discharged home having had intensive input from physiotherapy and the dietician.
    58→
    59→## Outcome and follow-up
    60→
    61→Owing to her unusually prolonged period of neutropenia and thrombocytopenia, and the associated marked mucositis and hair loss after only one cycle of chemotherapy, the patient's blood was sent for genetic analysis of dihydropyrimidine dehydrogenase (DPD) gene mutations. The results revealed she was heterozygous for the DPD gene mutation IVS14+1G>A. This mutation leads to decreased activity of the enzyme responsible for the metabolism of 5-FU, resulting in the build-up of toxic levels of 5-FU, responsible for her unusually severe reaction to the FOLFIRINOX chemotherapy regimen.
    62→
    63→Following discharge from hospital the patient was subsequently seen in the oncology outpatients. In view of the known DPD mutation, a change in her chemotherapy treatment was required and she was switched to single agent gemcitabine.[3](#R3) However, after 3 months of treatment she symptomatically deteriorated and a CT scan confirmed disease progression. She was thus switched to a combination treatment with gemcitabine and oxaliplatin and continues to be on this treatment 9 months after the initial diagnosis of metastatic pancreas cancer.[4](#R4)
    64→
    65→## Discussion
    66→
    67→The liver enzyme DPD is the initial rate-limiting enzyme responsible for the catabolism of ∼ 85% of all administered 5-FU. The reminder 15% is accountable for the cytotoxic effect which inhibits DNA and RNA synthesis, conferring the desired therapeutic effect.
    68→
    69→In individuals with partially inactive DPD enzyme, the levels of cytotoxic 5-FU metabolite can build up to toxic levels. These patients have an increased risk of developing life-threatening neutropenia and the onset of toxicity often occurs twice as fast compared with patients with normal DPD activity. To date, 39 different genetic mutations and polymorphisms have been identified with the most common being the IVS14+1G>A mutation, as was the case in our patient.[5](#R5)
    70→
    71→Currently, it is not routine to test for DPD enzyme activity prior to commencement of fluoropyrimidine therapy. In terms of measuring the levels of enzyme activity this would be highest in the liver but a liver biopsy is not without its own risks. Peripheral blood lymphocyte DPD levels may be used as a surrogate marker but methods are still laborious and importantly the absolute levels of DPD do not always correlate with the severity of 5-FU toxicity. Furthermore, routine genetic mutational analysis is flawed since many mutations are not associated with change in enzyme activity.[6](#R6)
    72→
    73→## Footnotes
    74→
    75→## References
    76→
    77→1. Phillips R, Hancock B, Graham J, et al. Prevention and management of neutropenic sepsis in patients with cancer: summary of NICE guidance. BMJ 345:e5368.  [DOI](https://doi.org/10.1136/bmj.e5368) | [PubMed](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/22993392/) | [Google Scholar](https://scholar.google.com/scholar_lookup?journal=BMJ&title=Prevention%20and%20management%20of%20neutropenic%20sepsis%20in%20patients%20with%20cancer:%20summary%20of%20NICE%20guidance&author=R%20Phillips&author=B%20Hancock&author=J%20Graham&volume=345&pages=e5368&pmid=22993392&doi=10.1136/bmj.e5368&)
    78→
    79→2. Conroy T, Desseigne F, Ychou M, et al. FOLFIRINOX versus gemcitabine for metastatic pancreatic cancer. N Engl J Med;364:1817–25  [DOI](https://doi.org/10.1056/NEJMoa1011923) | [PubMed](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/21561347/) | [Google Scholar](https://scholar.google.com/scholar_lookup?journal=N%20Engl%20J%20Med&title=FOLFIRINOX%20versus%20gemcitabine%20for%20metastatic%20pancreatic%20cancer&author=T%20Conroy&author=F%20Desseigne&author=M%20Ychou&volume=364&pages=1817-25&pmid=21561347&doi=10.1056/NEJMoa1011923&)
    80→
    81→3. Burris HA, III, Moore MJ, Andersen J, et al. Improvements in survival and clinical benefit with gemcitabine as first-line therapy for patients with advanced pancreas cancer: a randomized trial. J Clin Oncol 1997;15:2403–13  [DOI](https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.1997.15.6.2403) | [PubMed](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/9196156/) | [Google Scholar](https://scholar.google.com/scholar_lookup?journal=J%20Clin%20Oncol&title=Improvements%20in%20survival%20and%20clinical%20benefit%20with%20gemcitabine%20as%20first-line%20therapy%20for%20patients%20with%20advanced%20pancreas%20cancer:%20a%20randomized%20trial&author=HA%20Burris&author=MJ%20Moore&author=J%20Andersen&volume=15&publication_year=1997&pages=2403-13&pmid=9196156&doi=10.1200/JCO.1997.15.6.2403&)
    82→
    83→4. Brus C, Saif MW. Second line therapy for advanced pancreatic adenocarcinoma: where are we and where are we going? Highlights from the ‘2010 ASCO Annual Meeting’. Chicago, IL, USA. June 4–8, 2010. J Periodontol 2010;11:321–3  [PubMed](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/20601802/) | [Google Scholar](https://scholar.google.com/scholar_lookup?journal=J%20Periodontol&title=Second%20line%20therapy%20for%20advanced%20pancreatic%20adenocarcinoma:%20where%20are%20we%20and%20where%20are%20we%20going?%20Highlights%20from%20the%20%E2%80%982010%20ASCO%20Annual%20Meeting%E2%80%99.%20Chicago,%20IL,%20USA.%20June%204%E2%80%938,%202010&author=C%20Brus&author=MW%20Saif&volume=11&publication_year=2010&pages=321-3&pmid=20601802&)
    84→
    85→5. van Kuilenburg AB. Dihydropyrimidine dehydrogenase and the efficacy and toxicity of 5-fluorouracil. Eur J Cancer 2004;40:939–50  [DOI](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ejca.2003.12.004) | [PubMed](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/15093568/) | [Google Scholar](https://scholar.google.com/scholar_lookup?journal=Eur%20J%20Cancer&title=Dihydropyrimidine%20dehydrogenase%20and%20the%20efficacy%20and%20toxicity%20of%205-fluorouracil&author=AB%20van%20Kuilenburg&volume=40&publication_year=2004&pages=939-50&pmid=15093568&doi=10.1016/j.ejca.2003.12.004&)
    86→
    87→6. Yen JL, McLeod HL. Should DPD analysis be required prior to prescribing fluoropyrimidines? Eur J Cancer 2007;43:1011–16  [DOI](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ejca.2007.01.030) | [PubMed](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/17350823/) | [Google Scholar](https://scholar.google.com/scholar_lookup?journal=Eur%20J%20Cancer&title=Should%20DPD%20analysis%20be%20required%20prior%20to%20prescribing%20fluoropyrimidines?&author=JL%20Yen&author=HL%20McLeod&volume=43&publication_year=2007&pages=1011-16&pmid=17350823&doi=10.1016/j.ejca.2007.01.030&)
